We have 3 entities: Car, House and User. In order to create a user, we will need to provide his Car's ID and his House's ID. There is no list operation available to get both his House and his Car in the same request, as suggested in jd's use case above for Metric.

in my opinion, this is a bug, or at least an enhancement gdbgui should do. 

ideally, gdbgui's command prompt should behave exactly the same as the native gdb's command prompt.

but in the above case, it doesn't.

the problem here is that gdbgui assumes that gdb's commands are not interactive. you provide a command, and you get an output.

but in the above case, the command in interactive. gdb will response to the initial command with a question, for which, you need to advice, answer yes or no.

but gdbgui treats the answer "y" as an invalid command input.


plus, the way I set breakpoints in an unloaded library should work, if it were the native gdb.

Bringing across my most useful comment after I had dug into this issue:



Sorry, fell off the radar. I've just dug into it, and I think the easiest sensible fix would be in ipdb. I fixed it in my local copy by making the set_trace() function look like this:

```python
def set_trace(frame=None):
    update_stdout()
    wrap_sys_excepthook()
    if frame is None:
        frame = sys._getframe().f_back
    p = Pdb(def_colors)
    p.set_trace(frame)
    p.shell.restore_sys_module_state()
```

The last line is the key bit - that will put sys.modules['__main__'] back to its previous value.

Perhaps we shouldn't be replacing the __main__ module when InteractiveShell is merely instantiated, but that would require much more delicate changes to address.

The issue is that the `__package__` variable is not getting properly set to `None` per [PEP  366](http://legacy.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0366/).  It appears like this is specifically a Python 3 bug because the problem also appears in `pdb` but only in Python 3.  I've opened an issue [here](http://bugs.python.org/issue25682).

As a work around, you can either use Python 2 or simply inject `__package__ = None` at the top of your script while debugging.

I think time.sleep in the main thread does not work since the child thread receive the SIGCHLD signal (it is the parent process of the child process). Python writes a byte to the wakeup fd, but the main thread is not watching this fd, so it continue to sleep, and you receive the signal when the sleep returns. We had this issue in vdsm, and we solved it using signal.set_wakeup_fd, which works reliably, and nothing is fundamentally wrong with it.

See https://github.com/oVirt/vdsm/blob/master/lib/vdsm/common/sigutils.py.

Here is a simplified example:

```
$ cat test.py 
import fcntl
import logging
import os
import select
import signal
import subprocess
import threading


def sigchld(signo, frame):
    logging.info("received singal: %d", signo)


def set_non_blocking(fd):
    flags = fcntl.fcntl(fd, fcntl.F_GETFL)
    flags |= os.O_NONBLOCK
    fcntl.fcntl(fd, fcntl.F_SETFL, flags)


def run_child():
    logging.info("starting child...")
    subprocess.call(["sleep", "1"])
    logging.info("child terminated")


logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO,
                    format="%(asctime)s (%(threadName)s) %(message)s")

signal.signal(signal.SIGCHLD, sigchld)

pipe = os.pipe()
set_non_blocking(pipe[0])
set_non_blocking(pipe[1])

signal.set_wakeup_fd(pipe[1])

t = threading.Thread(target=run_child)
t.start()

logging.info("waiting 10 seconds...")
r, _, _ = select.select([pipe[0]], [], [], 10.0)
if not r:
    logging.info("timeout, no signal received")
logging.info("terminating")
```

Here is example run:

```
$ python test.py 
2017-03-20 22:15:25,631 (Thread-1) starting child...
2017-03-20 22:15:25,632 (MainThread) waiting 10 seconds...
2017-03-20 22:15:26,633 (MainThread) received singal: 17
2017-03-20 22:15:26,634 (Thread-1) child terminated
2017-03-20 22:15:26,634 (MainThread) terminating
```

This issue exist only with SIGCHLD from child processes started on another thread. Signals sent to the process can be handled with much less code.

I guess it might make sense to make lock acquisition a guaranteed schedule point, if we make a blanket rule that all potentially blocking operations are schedule points. But yeah, it's always going to be possible to write weird code that blocks the kernel -- the obvious example is just
```python
async def worker():
    while True:
         pass
```
:-)

So the question is more about coming up with more-nuanced guidance to users, so that they can look at a piece of code and make a clear prediction about whether it will be a problem or not, and where usually the answer is "not a problem".

Yea the sideloading needs some work. Currently it us only really useful when including a linked attribute, for example Organizations. This is because the sideloaded object is cached,  preventing an extra API call when accessing the linked attribute. As an example, the following code does not need to generate an extra API call for the organization:

```python
for user in zenpy_client.users(include=['organizations']):
    print(user.organization)
```
```
DEBUG - GET: https://d3v-zenpydev.zendesk.com/api/v2/users.json?include=organizations - {'timeout': 60.0}
DEBUG - GenericZendeskResponseHandler matched: {u'count': 1, ....
DEBUG - Caching: [Organization(id=115219725952)]
DEBUG - Caching: [User(id=116514121092)]
DEBUG - Cache HIT: [Organization 115219725952]
Organization(id=115219725952)
```

Whereas the following does:

```python
for user in zenpy_client.users():
    print(user.organization)
```

Hi, I took a look at your code, but I don't see how this can be integrated into ldap3. It uses a different asn1 library (ldap3 uses pyasn1 for sending and a custom faster asn1 decoder for received messages) and messages are built in a different way. I will play with it to understand how it works.

The asn.1 syntax is:


PolicyHintsRequestValue ::= SEQUENCE {      Flags    INTEGER  }

So you have to set the controlvalue accordingly, a printableString is not valid here because you’re sending the “0x1” value as a string 

You can check the pyasn.1 docs to build a Sequence made up of only an Integer.

* https proxying
* connection: close response header (in my case, in response to a connection: keep-alive request header, though I'm not sure that's relevant)
* urllib3/requests

@faststare My question would be why not using SQLite altogether? There are good reasons for using TinyDB with a JSON backend or using SQLite, but trying to combine both doesn't seem to bring any real benefits as far as I can see. I don't see how a JSON file clutters disk space more than SQLite does. If disk space is a concern, you always can write a storage that GZIPs the JSON file…

> I think this is something that's worthwhile to have in "core"

Basically this is a design question. I agree that this feature will be useful but from a design perspective I would prefer this to be an extension (something like `tinydb-datetime`). The main reason is that IMO the core should be kept as small as possible while offering comprehensive support for extensions of all kinds.

Maybe we could add some kind of support for custom encoders/decoders to TinyDB so users can use it without hassle. This could look like this:

```python
class DatetimeSerializer(Serializer):
    name = 'TinyDate'
    obj_class = datetime

    def encode(self, obj):
        # ...

    def decode(self, s):
        # ...

# ...
db.register_serializer(DatetimeSerializer)
```

Looking at the [docs](http://telepot.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference.html#telepot.helper.Answerer.answer) on the method `Answerer.answer()`, the compute function may return one of the following:

- a list of `InlineQueryResult`
- a tuple whose first element is a list of `InlineQueryResult`, **followed by positional arguments** to be supplied to `answerInlineQuery()`
- a dictionary representing **keyword arguments** to be supplied to `answerInlineQuery()`

So, to pass a `cache_time` to `answerInlineQuery()`, the compute function can return either of these:

- a tuple: `([result1, result2, ...], cache_time)`
- a dictionary: `{'results': [result1, result2, ...], 'cache_time': 999}`

Note: If importing colorama when stdout is captured by FDs, and `init()` is called, colors will be set to black on black at program end, even without printing anything.

In other words, the following code will set terminal colors to black on black:
```python
import sys
import os
from tempfile import TemporaryFile


# Capture stdout using file descriptors
stdoutfd = 1
saved_fd = os.dup(stdoutfd)
f = TemporaryFile()
os.dup2(f.fileno(), stdoutfd)

# Import colorama with captured stdout
import colorama
colorama.init()

# Restore stdout
os.dup2(saved_fd, stdoutfd)
os.close(saved_fd)
f.close()
```

To see how the code handles them I would suggest enabling the debug output and then running some samples thru - you will get a view into how each of the parsing routines identify and then handle ranges.

Debug mode is enabled by setting the `debug` module variable to `True` at https://github.com/bear/parsedatetime/blob/master/parsedatetime/__init__.py#L67

```
import parsedatetime as pdt

pdt.debug = True
...
```

TL;DR for anyone seeing this issue: ICU uses punctuated month abbreviations (e.g. *aug.*) which wreak havoc on date parsing. The problem does not affect locales built in to parsedatetime because the abbreviations are not punctuated.

Ok, I've tracked the problem back to the values stored in `Polygon`. The issue is that the value `0` is stored by SymPy as a `sympy.Zero` object. These `sympy.Zero` values are not accepted by `polyclipper.scale_to_clipper()` and so are raising the `TypeError` when checked.

To avoid the issue I can generate `new_list` casting `pt.x` and `pt.y` to float:

     new_list = [(float(pt.x), float(pt.y)) for pt in as_poly.vertices]

I'm not sure whether this is something that should be fixed in `sympy` or in `pyclipper`, or if the user should be expected to know that they need to make the conversion.

One option might be to convert anything that evaluates to `0` to `int(0)` in `pyclipper.scale_to_clipper()`. That would avoid the problem for this case, and for any other strange representations of `0` that may exist in other libraries.

So I was having a think about the whole privacy stuff and decided that it was an interesting topic.

py3status would easily leak any information that you encode eg if you use logging, notifications, put it in your format string.  So I've updated this PR to stop this leakage.  It is not an easy problem as we need to ensure that modules work, especially if they use external libs eg requests.

This implementation allows the value of 'private' config options only to be known to modules with the same name as the config option is given for.  And when used outside this `***` is used as the value.

All unicode methods should be available and work both when valid and not.

The whole thing is academic as all I need is to add a module that monkey patches this out and I can get the real values.  So this is more about keeping things semi-private and a bit of fun.

As well as `:base64` I have added `:hide` that is plain text in the config but will not leak to logs, i3bar etc

I think the issue is that this snippet you removed and the new stable sorting code are doing the opposite thing - the stable sort always puts a note on event after a note off event when they have the same tick, and this snippet always puts the note off after the note on.  The initial justification for putting note offs second is to avoid a stuck note (e.g. a note-on with no corresponding note-off).  I think the justification behind putting note-ons second is to prevent the artificial removal of a note.  Does that make sense/are you with me?

I think the actual correct thing to do would be to determine the ordering based on whether a note-on appears before or not.  For example, in this scenario:
```
O ------- O/F ------- F ...
```
where "O" is a note on and "F" is a note off and "O/F" is a note on and note off with the same tick (all notes having the same pitch of course), we should put the "F" first, because then we have two complete notes.  In this scenario:
```
O ----- F ------ O/F ------- O ------ F ---- ...
```
we should really just remove both the note on and the note off event with the same tick, because they correspond to a note which has zero duration.

Does this all make sense?  Also would be great to have @douglaseck's input.

We **were** using iptools for validating user input into a system as it was being setup to make sure that they were entering in a valid netmask. If the user does not enter in a valid netmask their system will have to be reconfigured. Our hope was that iptools would validate that the user had entered in a correct netmask. It does not. By auto-filling with 0's we cannot accurately validate whether or not a user has "fat-fingered" and possibly made a mistake when entering their netmask. 

255 may be a valid value for short-hand notation, however simply checking whether or not the value entered is a valid netmask would seem to me to be better suited to not having it auto-fill with zeros.

@bj0 yeah, probably, but we need to allocate unique channel IDs, which is a lot easier with a centralized service.. it can probably be done reliably-enough in a DHT, but would take more work and more time, increasing latency. Let's use #72 to brainstorm about DHT options.

@FrantzelasG I forgot to mention NAT.. most of the time, when I'm sending a file to someone else, neither of us has a public IP address, so we need *something* that we can both connect to, just to bootstrap the hole-punching process. And I've found that most of my machines that *do* have a publically-reachable IP address aren't really personal machines, they're servers, and they've got stable public hostnames too, and I just remember the hostname.

I agree that using standard ports is fine, so we needn't worry about fitting those extra characters into the string.

Using base36 (or 62 or whatever) loses a lot of the error-checking options for the code: any typo will prevent the connection from being made. (In the current `4-purple-sausages` scheme, the "4" is critical and has no error-checking, but the "purple" and "sausages" come from a short wordlist and so the receiving client could use the normal spell-checking techniques to find the closest word and tolerate typos). So I'd like to make the non-error-checked portion as short as possible.

The `purple-sausages` part of the existing scheme is basically a one-time password (although the PAKE crypto we use makes it safe even if someone guesses the code afterwards). Using a pre-shared key or some out-of-band thing like port-knocking means you have to deliver that secret along with the rest of the code, making it bigger and more error-prone (e.g. "send me a file, by typing in IP address X.Y.Z.Q, and also make a TCP connection to port 1948 just before you run the program, and also pre-encrypt the file with key abc..256..bits..later..xyz", vs "4-purple-sausages"). And, if it's optional, then people will prefer to not use it, and then this isn't a secure file-transfer tool anymore.

My other concern with transcribing an IP address is that the direction of the file transfer might not align with the direction of the information transfer. If I'm sending a file to you, and you've got a public IP address (but I don't), then first you have to tell me your IP address, and then I send you the file. That's backwards: if I'm sending the file to you, then I want to be telling you some string, not the other way around. They might line up if I happen to have a public IP (then I tell you "please contact this IP and ask for a file") but there's no guarantee that it'll work out that way.

And yeah, v6 makes all manual-adress-handling things harder. I'm glad there are folks doing things like automatic-local-service-discovery (MDNS-type stuff), that's going to make things a lot better.

Yup, the wormhole code consists of the nameplate (aka channel id aka the numeric part), and the secret words. The entropy comes from the words, since the nameplate is necessarily revealed to the server. The nameplate is allocated from a server-managed pool, which randomly chooses the shortest available number (so if there are fewer than 10 channels open, it will return a single-digit nameplate).

The recipient (and attacker) gets exactly one guess per nameplate, and the attacker's chance of success depends entirely upon the entropy of the words (the number of nameplates doesn't figure into it).

I'm guessing this concern is resolved.. I'm going to close this, but I'll try to improve the docs to make it more clear what's going on, since I've heard this concern from multiple people. Obviously we're not explaining it very well :).

You can get some idea of the issues of switching it on by commenting out this line (https://github.com/peterbrittain/asciimatics/blob/master/asciimatics/screen.py#L1724), which hides the terminal cursor, and running the existing samples. Your proposal would require some very fragile code to detect those precise conditions and temporarily re-enable the cursor.  I don't think I have the spare time to get that working perfectly (and it would probably keep breaking as people invent ever more cunning ways to use the package)...

How about an option to use a solid cursor instead of a flashing one instead?  That would remove the animation and so also fix any CPU drain.  The default would be to animate the cursor for backwards compatibility.

Agree, not the place to discuss actually adding other formats, but wanted to bring it up so that this solution *could* accommodate other formats in the future.

You could do something like
``` python
>>> from somepackage import dumperfunc
>>> frontmatter.dumps(post, dump_func=lambda x: dumperfunc(x, foo=1, bar=2, quux=3))
```
which would provide full power to the user, but I agree, wouldn't let you provide defaults to 'supported' external libraries (like pyaml)

You can of course modify a `dict` passed in the kwargs, so 
``` python
def dumps(post, dump_func, **kwargs):
   kwargs=kwargs.copy() 
   if dump_func==pyaml.dump: # obvs would need to do this in a way that doesn't rely on pyaml being available
       if 'foo' not in kwargs:
          kwargs['foo']='defaultfoo'
          ..snip snip...
  
   dump_func(post.metadata, **kwargs)
```

(Would be a fairly natural extension to add a sep='---' / sep='+++' arg at some point in the future to denote different dump types, but we're not talking about that here ;))

@idvorkin  yes, that is a limit on the ring's doorbell API, however, you can workaround this issue. 

You can use the doorbell.history(limit=100, older_than=<oldest_object_returned_from_first_limit>). 

``` 
es = [e for e in doorbell.history(limit=100)]
new = [e for e in doorbell.history(limit=100, older_than=es[-1])]
```

That would do the trick.

A few points first:

1. This library is essentially just a set of CouchDB/Cloudant convenience API wrapped around Python [requests](http://docs.python-requests.org/en/master/).  
2. We do not currently support use with CouchDB 2.0 because adequate testing has just not been performed yet.  

Keeping these two points in mind, this library does lend itself nicely to work with any CouchDB or Cloudant like HTTP request endpoint, mainly because of point (1).  In other words if the end points in CouchDB and Cloudant match then there really is no reason why you shouldn't be able to use a CloudantDatabase object to interact with CouchDB instead of Cloudant.  The only limitation there is that you can't use Admin Party mode when setting up a `Cloudant` client.  [CloudantDatabase](http://python-cloudant.readthedocs.io/en/latest/database.html#cloudant.database.CloudantDatabase) just provides an additional set of convenience methods on top of what a [CouchDatabase](http://python-cloudant.readthedocs.io/en/latest/database.html#cloudant.database.CouchDatabase) provides.  So you should be able to construct a Query object by passing it a CouchDatabase object or a CloudantDatabase object that actually points to a CouchDB instance.  The Query object doesn't care.

With all this said, the [Query](http://python-cloudant.readthedocs.io/en/latest/query.html) class is a wrapper for the `_find` endpoint (also see the CloudantDatabase [get_query_result](http://python-cloudant.readthedocs.io/en/latest/database.html#cloudant.database.CloudantDatabase.get_query_result) convenience method).  The `_search` endpoint is handled by the CloudantDatabase [get_search_result](http://python-cloudant.readthedocs.io/en/latest/database.html#cloudant.database.CloudantDatabase.get_search_result) convenience method.  Have a look at the documentation and see which convenience API is the right one for you.

The hang is caused by LINGER behavior on `socket_task`. At the beginning of every loop, `socket_task` is sending a ping to request new tasks. The last request from one or more workers is going out after the producer has closed its socket. This means that the request for a new task is waiting in a queue on the workers. The default LINGER behavior is to block Context.term until undelivered messages can be sent. If you set `socket_task.linger = 0`, it will immediately discard unsent messages.

If you change

```python
socket_task = context.socket(zmq.REQ)
```

to

```python
socket_task = context.socket(zmq.REQ)
socket_task.linger = 0
```

you should be set.

I encountered the same issue trying to build on Cygwin with GCC, I got around this (and other cygwin specific issues) adding the following to the top-level CMakeLists.txt:

SET(CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS "${CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS} -fvisibility=hidden -Wall **-Wa,-mbig**-obj -**std=gnu++11**" )

-Wa,-mbig-obj tells the assembler it's gonna be a big file, I suspect Mingw requires the same flag but I haven't tested that yet, if I get around doing that (probably in a month) and to the bottom of this issue I can offer these changes as a pull request.

std=gnu++11 helps cygwin find POSIX functions like strncasecmp and realpath under Windows.

I explored this a bit, and the following works in my `tox.ino`:

```
[testenv]
# We must specify deps, in order to get tox to run the install_command
deps = pipenv
# Allow using sh from outside the virtualenv
whitelist_externals = sh
# Use sh to allow running multiple commands. First run pip to install
# pipenv (and any other dependencies), then run pipenv to install stuff
# from the pipfile. This passes a dummy argument to sh to fill $0 and
# the list of packages (from deps=) as $@.
install_command = sh -c 'pip install "$@" && pipenv install --ignore-pipfile --deploy --system --dev' dummy {packages}

commands = actual_test_command
```

It would be very nice if it is possible to know where the environment would be before hand, without being stuck with `./.venv`.[#] The problem here, however, is that this is already established, and already working. To offset that, you’d need to provide both a very convincing argument (you’re getting there, but I don’t think this is enough to convince all), *and a working alternative*. [#]: *Technically* the virtual environment name is predictable. It is not random at all. *But* in practice it is not very useful because there is no public API to get this information before the creation.

@ncoghlan that's right:

> Right but it won’t get parsed in a resolution graph until the package is installed afaik

Pip-tools handles parsing by using a `pip.index.PackageFinder` instance to ultimately generate a `pip.req.req_set.RequirementSet` from which `pip.req.req_install.InstallRequirement` instances are generated. Since this all runs in the host interpreter, each step needs to be fooled into believing that it is working with the 'guest' python version to do resolution properly, while not actually performing any installation operations because they will fail.

So far based on the call graph I can identify the following pieces that need to be fooled:

* `PIP_PYTHON_PATH`
* `PIP_PYTHON_VERSION`
* `pip.index.PackageFinder.valid_tags` which comes from `pip.pep425tags.get_supported`
* `sys.path`
* `sys.version`
* `sysconfig` calls are made in `distlib.wheel`

If at any point those resolve to the wrong interpreter (i.e. the host interpreter), resolution runs the risk of failing.  So now I'm wondering if we shouldn't just 'install' a resolver script into the virtualenv and run it in that context and just dump the output to the host interpreter via a subprocess or something.

The issue I was trying to solve with the `-I` flag is that in one of the builds I am working with, for some reason if the build breaks, the `bin/buildout` script is removed.

Running `pip install -Ur requirements.txt` proved to not be enough to recreate the script in the bin folder, because the package is already in the site packages folder.

Now I understand that, if I want the build to be as much convergent as possible, I have to run first `pip install -Ur requirements.txt && pip install -Ir requirements.txt`.

In the buildout issue mentioned just above, @dholth had this to say about adding wheel installation support to setuptools:

> It should be easy to add wheel install support to `setuptools` or `easy_install` or whatever `buildout` may need. `pkg_resources` was the first tool to support dist-info directories, and once they are unpacked they look exactly like eggs in your `pkg_resources.working_set`. There's code from the `wheel install` command that could be called and is fairly setuptools-onic. Use `wheel.install.WheelFile()` instances which are sortable by how desirable the particular wheel is, this works with only the filename. Grab WheelFile.install_paths(), change them all to point to the single directory in which it should be installed egg-style, pass to WheelFile.install(overrides={...}), then generate console scripts as usual.

I guess this would mean vendoring wheel just, like `packaging`.

> The only part that might need a little love is `wheel.pep425tags` which generates a slightly different list of supported tags than `pip.pep425tags`, mainly pip supports more OSX tags. They should probably just be kept identical.

The `pep425tags` part should probably be treated by `packaging` somehow, so that both `pip` and `setuptools` (and `wheel`?) treat the compatibility tags the same. Currently in setuptools, the compatibility tags are handled by `pkg_resources.Environment.can_add()` AFAICT, and for the purposes of supporting wheel, and treating it as just as downloadable as any other distribution, we might not need to change anything, though we might want to defer to an official `pep425tags` implementation in the future.

As for vendoring wheel, we need to weight the cost of that versus the benefit of using it for just copying some files around (and the cost of duplicating this code into another, potentially different, implementation).

`'bias'` is a feature that capture the proportion of a given label in the training set.

Intuitively, if you have no other feature than `'bias'` in your model (so your features are just indicator functions of the current label) then the weight of the feature learned will be higher if the label appears more. When you will predict, you will just always return the label with higher weight, which is the one which appear the most during training.

In a 'real' CRF, it is just a way to express that some label are rare by themself and other not, so you may take count of this (for example you can imagine a language in which verbs are mostly avoided and not nouns, so you should express that with the weight of the `'bias'` feature lower for verbs labels than nouns).

While the cause has been identified, fixing it is not easy.

`nupic.core` has been changed a lot since the time I integrated google test and coverall. It has advanced to become installable as a standalone library, and from what I observed, the distributed binary of `nupic.core`(as pip package `nupic.bindings` and on s3) is also built by travis-ci, so it has to be a release version.

Coverage instrument means a lot of stub code into the binary, contains a lot of information about files, functions, branches and lines, which will affect the binary size and performance. Thus in order to bring the coverage functionality back, we need an extra debug version.

The ability of compiling a debug version of `nupic.core` exists: the `CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE` option which survived to date. We could add coverage related compiler flags back when `CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE` is `Debug`. We also need to link the tests with the debug version of `nupic.core`.

The above approach feels not intuitive, and vulnerable to future maintenance. Here's a better way to do this:

*  Remove coverage from all the current builds, leave them to build the release version
* Add a separate build(preferably clang) with a separate environment variable, to build debug version of `nupic.core` and run tests and report coverage to coveralls.

@antigenius - yes, that's correct.  However the json deserialized does not equal a cstruct.

Let's say you had '{"x":3}' as the body.  The `simplejson` will deserialize that into `{'x': 3}` which is not a valid cstruct.  Colander's cstruct has only strings so calling colander's deserialize on `{'x': 3}` will probably choke.  If you serialize `{'x': 3}` with colander you get `{'x': '3'}` which is the cstruct format.

> Do we really want any generic Invenio production site to be slowed down by deprecation alerts?

> [...]

> we don't have the resources to fix all them (not even Invenio has existing replacement for all the technologies we are deprecating(!))

Here are my 2 cents on this topic.

As we are splitting Invenio into many packages, and as Invenio v2.1+ is becoming literally a framework of independent components, it will often happen that some Invenio v1.x facility does not have its counterpart in v2.1 and above yet.  Installations needing these features can run Invenio v2.0 which contains fully all the legacy modules.  Installations not needing these features can progressively upgrade to v2.1, v2.2, v2.3, etc, as we shall be cleaning the Invenio core and progressively releasing modules as separate packages.  This may bring many, many deprecation warnings along the way in the coming months, before v3.0 will be finally out.

From the point of view of Invenio being more and more a Pythonic library, instead of a monolithic ILS application, it seems natural to emit deprecation warnings by default, and relying on people switching them out on their production system.  After all, we don't recommend to run Invenio v2.x in production yet; and people testing it out always start on their developer instances anyway.  So, if we provide clear documentation on how to run Invenio in production -- which necessarily passes by creating an overlay -- it seems enough to me to simply document in big fat letters how people can switch various warnings off on their production systems, if they want to.  But, since Invenio v2.x is marketed as a developer version, it seems consistent to me to emit as much warnings as possible, so that services are informed early and can catch and fix any troubles as soon as possible. When the time comes that we shall start recommending Invenio v2.N for production one day, we could perhaps consider switching the default the other way...  The question is, has the time come already to flip the switch that way?  Or not yet?

P.S. It may be interesting to note that Python itself was showing deprecation warnings by default in 2.6 and earlier versions, but as of 2.7 it is not showing them anymore, unless people explicitly ask for them via CLI switch. https://docs.python.org/2/using/cmdline.html#cmdoption-W

The endpoint configuration is parsed by a Marshmallow schema which creates a config object. This config handles endpoint vs default values, thus the defaults can be overriden in deposit by replacing the schema class.

The schema is used by the extension's `init_app` and the result is given to the `create_blueprint` function. This enables to change the configuration schema and result classes and still use `create_blueprint`.

The config is given to each View class. It is then added to the application context on each REST request thanks to the decorator `with_endpoint_config`. The endpoint_config can be retrieved from anywhere by using `config_loader.current_endpoint_config`.

There is one thing I don't know, it is if we use the endpoint_config for everything. For example I started removing the `links_factory` from the serializers arguments. The base serializer can directly retrieve the `links_factory` by accessing `config_loader.current_endpoint_config`. Is this something we want?

It is nice that people are referring to the actual documents to decide that it works as intended, but let's stick to what one of the early proponents of JWT decided (https://auth0.com/docs/refresh-token). This means that while an REFRESH token is unexpired (!! independend of the original jwt), you can use the refresh token to refresh your jwt. This means that a quick fix is needed, in my humble opinion.

I'd like to hear further thoughts on this and reasons why to stick to the documentation.

Main reason for the behaviour such as described by https://auth0.com/docs/refresh-token is: You can have a really short duration for you JWT (let's say 15 minutes or less), so a really short amount of time for someone who hijacked a users token to use it, while still not requiring your users to login every 15 minutes. Only when the refresh token expires your users need to login again. The expiry for the refresh token can then be set to for example 1 year.

Although one can argue how much more safe it is (you should be using https anyway, and if someone breaks into the client app, why wouldn't they steal the refresh token as well?) at least this behaviour makes sense. Otherwise the whole refresh token part can just as well be left out in my opinion...

Use case: I was generating C code for an arduino, and to get a C variable name for a URIRef, I used `'pwm%s' % (hash(dev) % 99999)` and hoped for the best. (And didn't think about negative numbers, apparently.) My system was rebuilding and reuploading on every run, since the generated C code didn't have the same checksum each time. Nothing was broken; it just ran slow. hash(str(dev)) is my workaround in python2. 

Probably I should use hashlib.md5(dev).hexdigest() if I want a stable hash, or 'pwm_'+dev.encode('base64').strip('=\n') if I want to try something that's properly unique for each URI. Or re.sub(r'[^0-9a-zA-Z]', '_', dev) for something that's probably unique and also readable.

set up SQLAlchemy to use declarative extension with Serializable Base
```
from sqlalchemy import create_engine
from sqlalchemy.orm import scoped_session, sessionmaker
from sqlalchemy.ext.declarative import declarative_base
from flask.ext.jsontools import JsonSerializableBase

engine = create_engine('postgresql://xxx:yyy@localhost:5432/mydatabase, convert_unicode=True) 
db_session = scoped_session(sessionmaker(bind=engine))

Base = declarative_base(cls=(JsonSerializableBase,))
Base.query = db_session.query_property()
```

The method you are using is not meant for that type of call which will retrieve a zip file containing a list of codes for time-series datasets in that database. To programmatically pull the file you could use something like:

```python
import requests

url = "https://www.quandl.com/api/v3/databases/FRED/codes"
api_key = "<your api key here>"
	
resp = requests.get(url, data={"api_key": api_key}, stream=True)
	
with open("FRED_codes.zip", "wb") as f:
	for chunk in resp.iter_content(chunk_size=1024):
		f.write(chunk)
		f.flush()
```

And then use a library like `zipfile` to extract the csv in the zip.

why were origin and bytecode removed, were they unused?

@pcostell : I think assigning `key` should be allowed so that you can effectively "copy" the data from one entity to another.... For example:

```python
entity = get_entity_somehow()

# "Copy" the data to another entity:
entity.key = Key('OtherKind', 'my-key-name')
entity.put()
```

Is that crazy?

---

I was also saying that `key` should be allowed as a name for something to store *not* the actual `Key`. That is... `entity['key'] = True` should be allowed, and datastore would just treat `key` as though it was any other property like `enabled`... 

If you want to set the entity's `Key` (do it with `Entity(key=Key(...))` or `entity.key = Key(...)` (not `entity['key'] = ...` which sets a property called key rather than the entity's key).

A 3 node Cloud Bigtable cluster can handle writing at least 30K rows per second, and each additional node adds 10K rows per second. [ We tested up to 3,500 nodes with Cloud Bigtable](https://cloudplatform.googleblog.com/2016/03/financial-services-firm-processes-25-billion-stock-market-events-per-hour-with-Google-Cloud-Bigtable.html) with a linear increase in performance.

The 800 rows per second is a limitation of the python client and what ever VM you're on.  The process sends 300 rows, and waits for a response.  You can run multiple processes that each read in a different file to improve total throughput against your cluster.

The Java client has an async client that performs far better in these situations, since it has a robust async client.  We have not ported that functionality to python yet.  [Here is an example](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/cloud-bigtable-examples/tree/master/java/simple-performance-test) with the Java version.

`google-cloud-pubsub==0.29.0`

```
import logging
import time
from google.cloud import pubsub_v1


class Subscriber():
    def __init__(self, project, topic, subscription, callback, max_messages=1):
        self.subscriber = pubsub_v1.SubscriberClient()
        self.subscription_path = self.subscriber.subscription_path(project, subscription)
        self.flow_control = pubsub_v1.types.FlowControl(max_messages=max_messages)
        self.logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
        self.callback = callback

    def run(self):
        self.logger.info('Initializing subscriber...')
        self.subscriber.subscribe(self.subscription_path, callback=self.callback, flow_control=self.flow_control)

        while True:
            time.sleep(60)
```

The problem was the subscriber was no longer pulling messages. Previously with blocking calls, if something went wrong with a pull command or anything else, if the exception wasn't handled the application would error out. Now that we're using this library, background threads erroring doesn't cause the main process to stop. How can I programmatically verify that the background threads are working correctly, in this case actually pulling from pubsub?

Some context on this update: this properly uses WheelFile.install to extract the contents of a wheel, and then copies that into the pex chroot. I think this is a much better solution than the first try.

The alternative approach, which I think would work, but be more brittle, would be to add a wheel-based finder (including an override of the ImpImporter from pkgutils). That would allow us to include the wheel, in its original pypi form, in the pex. The catch, and the reason why I prefer this approach, is that we'd basically be reproducing some logic from the pkgutils WheelFile.install in the finder, and that would require us to make sure that we update pex logic after any changes to pkgutils.  The approach in this change just uses wheelfile, and then pulls in the lesult.

It's worth noting that a release build of the native engine is large. The Linux .so is 65M, and the Mac .so is 15M. They zip down ok, so that a pex is only about 35M, but 35M is still very large. (I hadn't realised until now how different they were; I'm curious, but not quite curious enough to find out...)

It's likely that when https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/36342 is resolved and we can be building a cdylib not a hacky dylib, the linker can do a lot more stripping and these will get smaller, but platform-specific pexes would have a non-trivial size improvement (which maps to a non-trivial speed improvement on first run).

It's also possible (if we're talking about a way to easily define simple datatypes) it might be appropriate to apply this to options (e.g. a class could be declared which ensures its argument is an existing file/directory). While nothing would change from the pants user's point of view, it might be easier for implementors of subsystems and tasks to do appropriate error checking if we could match args to more complex types.

In general, yes. Invoking SASsession will make a new connection to whatever configuration you specify. Saying that, I will say that there is the potential for returning the existing connection that already exists if you are assigning the same object to a session:
sas1 = SASsession()
sas1 = SASsession()

as opposed to using different object:

sas1 = SASsession()
sas2 = SASsession()

In the SASsession code, I check to see if the object I am handed (sas1 or sas2 in the above examples) already has a connection established, and if so, I just return the object as is. When that object is deleted, by python, it drives my code to disconnect from, and shut down SAS. In the case of reassigning the same objec (sas1 -> sas1) I believe I have seen different python behavior across some versions. I used to see the object get deleted and then recreated, meaning I would see the disconnect of the original SAS session and then connect to a new one. Currently, I don't see the delete for this object until the python process is shuting down (I see this in the Jupyter notebook), But I also don't get the same actual object with the connection already established. So, a new SAS session is established and when the notebook is stopped, then I see both objects get deleted and their SAS sessions shut down.

I used to see it overlap, but it just depends on python garbage collection and other things I don't control. So, yes, each SASsession() invocation creates a new connection to a new SAS session.

    permissions: '0755'
    content: |
      #!/usr/bin/bash
      if [ "$1" != '--really' ]; then
        echo "command must be run as: $0 --really"
        exit 1
      fi
      # procedure requires the store-admin
      ADMIN_RUNNING=$(docker inspect --format="{{ .State.Running }}" deis-store-admin)
      if [ $? -eq 1 ] || [ "$ADMIN_RUNNING" == "false" ]; then
        echo "deis-store-admin container is required for graceful shutdown"
        exit 2
      fi
      set -e -x -o pipefail
      # determine osd id
      CURRENT_STATUS=$(docker exec deis-store-admin ceph health | awk '{print $1}')
      OSD_HOSTS=($(etcdctl ls /deis/store/hosts/| awk -F'/' '{print $5}'))
      for HOST in "${OSD_HOSTS[@]}"
      do
        PUBLIC_IP=$(fleetctl list-machines -fields="machine,ip" -full -no-legend| grep `cat /etc/machine-id` | awk '{print $2}')
        if [ "$HOST" = "$PUBLIC_IP" ] ; then
          OSD_ID=$(etcdctl get /deis/store/osds/$PUBLIC_IP)
          break
        fi
      done
      # if we own an osd and its healthy, try to gracefully remove it
      if [ ! -z "$OSD_ID" ] && [[ "$CURRENT_STATUS" == *"HEALTH_OK"* ]] && [ ${#OSD_HOSTS[@]} -gt "3" ]; then
        docker exec deis-store-admin ceph osd out $OSD_ID
        sleep 30
        TIMEWAITED=0
        until [[ $(docker exec deis-store-admin ceph health) == *"HEALTH_OK"* ]]
        do
          if [ $TIMEWAITED -gt "1200" ]
          then
            echo "ceph graceful removal timeout exceeded"
            break
          fi
          echo "waiting" && sleep 5
          TIMEWAITED=$((TIMEWAITED+5))
        done

> Do you think there two steps to implementing this: a first step keeping things in the string, to avoid https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/1589 , and then a version after that once https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/1589 is fixed?

Obviously, I'd prefer a design where https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/1589 is fixed. Then, version tags like `.vc14` in package names or version numbers will rarely be needed. It may still be a good idea to list feature tags in the build string, but only for informative purposes (so that users don't have to open the package in order to find out about features), not for version matching (as this is too fragile, given the second class nature of build strings).

However, if https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/1589 cannot be fixed before going 'all in' on features, feature tags in version numbers (or package names) are a great help for the version resolution algorithm. For example, a package that needs at least version 1.46 of boost would specify
```
requirements:
  run:
    - visual-studio 14.0
    - boost    >=1.46,*.vc14*
```
Thus, the version matching algorithm will only consider packages that have the right feature, because version number matching excludes everything else to begin with. Additional requirements (more features) can simply be concatenated with the 'and' (comma) operator. Using jinja2 templates, this generalizes into
```
requirements:
  run:
    - {{compiler.run_requirement}} 
    - boost    >=1.46,*{{compiler.version_tag}}*
```

Around line 406 of your trace is

```
TRACE conda.gateways.disk.create:_do_copy(252): copying /home/user/miniconda2/pkgs/xz-5.2.3-h5e939de_4/bin/xz => /DEEP_fhgfs/tmp/tmpUAg7af/4ad6953a-3e9e-488f-a59b-c4a708d60584
TRACE conda.core.path_actions:verify(386): rewriting prefixes in /DEEP_fhgfs/tmp/conda/xz/bin/xz
TRACE conda.gateways.disk.update:update_file_in_place_as_binary(35): in-place update path locked for /DEEP_fhgfs/tmp/tmpUAg7af/4ad6953a-3e9e-488f-a59b-c4a708d60584
```

and then around line 636

```
TRACE conda.core.path_actions:execute(411): linking /DEEP_fhgfs/tmp/tmpUAg7af/4ad6953a-3e9e-488f-a59b-c4a708d60584 => /DEEP_fhgfs/tmp/conda/xz/bin/xz
TRACE conda.gateways.disk.create:create_link(298): hard linking /DEEP_fhgfs/tmp/tmpUAg7af/4ad6953a-3e9e-488f-a59b-c4a708d60584 => /DEEP_fhgfs/tmp/conda/xz/bin/xz
```

What's happening here is that the `xz` executable is modified at install time with prefix rewrites.  This happens in two phases.

During the verification phase of the install process, we detect that the file is going to need prefix rewrites, so we copy it to a temporary location and do the rewrites there.  That's so that, before we enter the transaction, we'll know the exact final state of the file, including the sha256 sum for the file with the prefix modification.

During the second phase, which occurs *during* the transaction, we link the file from the temporary location into the environment.  Here, at least for many filesystems, hard-linking *should* be fine, because what we're hard-linking is the new inode we created in the temporary location.  However if the temporary location is on a different mount, then hard-linking will obviously fail, and we should fall back to copying.  That's exactly what we do here:

https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/c54d490419d79d07a618a2c3ddc0729f20560246/conda/gateways/disk/create.py#L294-L306

The log information shows that there's no hard-link failure happening on your system.  But conda most definitely thinks what it's created is a hard link and not a symbolic link for `/DEEP_fhgfs/tmp/conda/xz/bin/xz`.  Can you poke at `/DEEP_fhgfs/tmp/conda/xz/bin/xz` more and verify that it indeed ends up being a symbolic link once the conda operation is done?  This is definitely a puzzle now.

The 2 slightly different versions of the error messages you were seeing for throttle scenarios was due to the fact that sometimes throttle is detected at the gateway level and Gateway sends RequestTooLargerException(the second one that you are seeing). The first one is coming directly from the server and hence has a slightly different message but since both of them have status code 429, they are due to the same reason.

It will be a while until we implement the throttle retry policy for all documentdb APIs for Python SDK but you can take a look at http://stackoverflow.com/questions/29275447/exception-microsoft-azure-documents-requestratetoolargeexception-while-querying as an example how that's implement in .NET SDK, so you may have to do similar thing for Python. In short, you catch the exception, check if it's status code is 429, get the value of "RetryAfterInMilliseconds" property from the response header and wait until that many ms, and resubmit the request. You can loop for say 3 times before giving up.

Meanwhile, we should be able to modify the tests so that each one of them doesn't creates a new collection every time. We can have one or two test collections created which are shared by most of the tests for creating resources within that collection and cleaning them up at the end. Collection creates are the only one which are causing problems in this case, so if we can eliminate that we should be good. We may be able to get to this approach in our next release but if you are comfortable enough to make these changes, feel free to do so and we will accept it as a PR.

Regards,
Rajesh

@ellisonbg it's most likely that it was putting outputs on the page that was the real cost (especially if the symptom was a hung browser) rather than the network traffic, since the way we append stream output is *extremely* inefficient. This is the primary issue addressed in the js side of #506. If the network is slow or clogged, that should actually make it *harder* to crash the browser.

I think the scalable approach is to dump excess output to files on the server side, but that adds some peculiarities, since output is part of the kernel API, not necessarily related to notebooks (e.g. thebe has no file or path associated with the output messages). Because of this, it seems to me like overflow truncation should look like:

1. part of the Kernels service
2. overflowed output is associated with kernels, with a file per parent (i.e. output area)
3. overflowed output is only accessible while the kernel exists, and is deleted on shutdown of the kernel and/or server
4. On overflow, OutputArea presents a control that allows one-time, explicit "Yes I want this" fetch of overflowed output into the notebook.

If we want this really scalable, the only *true* answer is for notebooks to actually be multiple files, so we don't have to read the whole thing at once, and can scroll within outputs, having only a limited amount of content available in memory at a time. I don't think this is feasible with the current notebook format, though.

It seems like a key-value database would be a better fit than SQL for the signatures, though that's less clear for history. There are several embedded k-v databases, like dbm, Berkeley DB, Kyoto Cabinet. Most seem to have copyleft licenses; UnQLite is an exception, but probably has the same issues with NFS that SQLite does: https://twitter.com/unqlite_db/status/642479750368370688 . And I have no evidence that any of the other options *doesn't* fall over with NFS.

The only embeddable thing I've found that might work is the approach used in [cdb](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cdb_(software)) - to add an entry, rebuild the database and rename it over the old file, relying on rename to be atomic. But our database gets big enough and changes often enough that we probably don't want to be rewriting it over NFS on every addition.

So if we can't trust the filesystem, we're back to database server to maintain consistency. I still suspect it's worth looking at key-value stores over SQL databases, but I don't have the patience to wade through all the NoSQL hype and marketing to figure out which one would actually be a good fit.

It sounds like you're trying an import in two different installations of Python, or two different environments. Check `sys.executable` to see which Python and environment you're running in, and `sys.path` to see where it looks to import modules.

Python packages must be installed separately for each copy of Python you use, and if you are using virtualenvs or conda envs, packages must be installed into each environment where you need them. Either the package is not installed in one, or a different version of the package is installed.

To install packages to a particular Python installation or environment using pip, you can run it like this:

```shell
path/to/python -m pip install ...
# Replace path/to/python with the path of the Python executable (sys.executable)
```

If you are using conda environments, you can install packages from conda like this:

```shell
source activate myenv  # On Windows, just 'activate myenv' (no 'source')
conda install ...
```

For more detail, see [this blog post](http://takluyver.github.io/posts/i-cant-import-it.html).

For some background, I'm kind of coming at using Peru from two perspectives.

The developers I support would certainly be able to edit the file... but they're also inclined to gravitate to tools like http://wp-cli.org/ when they're available, and we use https://github.com/nvie/gitflow for just about every repo. Experience is telling me that some people do better with longer commands that follow some sentence-like construction, than they do with short commands that require extra cognitive overhead. `git flow feature $verb $name` has had far better uptake than "do `git checkout -b $name`, but only from `develop`, and prefix the branch name with `feature/`" etc, and I've had more work supporting them questions that get answered with "edit this configuration file in this way" than those involving "run this command, and it has a built-in 'help' thing". They're generally more self-sufficient with the latter.

And the shininess of CLIs with tools like Bower and NPM puts pressure on this kind thing here too.

As a SysAdmin, I'm also definitely looking for opportunities to script as many tasks as possible. Once the developers are done with a project, I get to maintain and update everything, which includes sucking down whatever changes a site's admin-users make to it. I can coerce my developers to edit `peru.yaml`, but no way can I get users who know nothing but a GUI to do so, let alone commit those changes. I also make pretty heavy use of tools like https://github.com/interconnectit/Search-Replace-DB that can require some scary-long commands.

I can think of a couple of options for generating/regenerating Peru's YAML that'd fit with my needs here, so this definitely isn't a "you're my only hope!" situation. Especially when making Peru write to parts of a file begs to break the world in fascinating ways.

context manager support is [implemented] (https://github.com/pyserial/pyserial/blob/master/serial/serialutil.py#L500), [see also](https://pyserial.readthedocs.org/en/latest/shortintro.html).

The port is opened when the name is specified:

```python
with serial.Serial("/dev/ttyUSB0") as s:
    s.write(b"hello")
```

or call `open()` later:
```python
with serial.Serial() as s:

Thank you for this, Sten! :) Just before Christmas, Scanpy/AnnData incorporated .loom support: https://scanpy.readthedocs.io, ([docs read](http://anndata.readthedocs.io/en/latest/anndata.read_loom.html#anndata.read_loom), [docs write](http://anndata.readthedocs.io/en/latest/anndata.AnnData.write_loom.html#anndata.AnnData.write_loom), [code read](https://github.com/theislab/anndata/blob/2965233eb1c4f50247af3437b6bd17520eb5233c/anndata/readwrite/read.py#L129-L150), [code write](https://github.com/theislab/anndata/blob/2965233eb1c4f50247af3437b6bd17520eb5233c/anndata/readwrite/write.py#L52-L63)) Unfortunately, still with the old loom version. We will work on making use of the possibilities of the new version, which will allow loosing less information when exporting to `.loom`.

What might be interesting for all efforts of storing sparse matrices in hdf5: I've worked on a more [basic way](https://falexwolf.de/blog/171212_sparse_matrices_with_h5py/) of interfacing sparse matrices in hdf5 files, which works a bit different than in loom, but provides some advantages and additional functionality.

We mentioned [before](https://github.com/linnarsson-lab/loompy/issues/1#issuecomment-344525924) that we planned to move from a static to a dynamically-backed AnnData object from the very beginning. We did in the past weeks. AnnData now offers both: convenience of fancy indexing and all what pandas/numpy offer for accessing and manipulating data ("memory mode") versus full hdf5-backing ("backed mode"), with reduced speed and possibilities of accessing and manipulating data. See my thoughts on this [here](https://falexwolf.de/blog/171223_AnnData_indexing_views_HDF5-backing/). Given that loom [didn't satisfy](https://github.com/linnarsson-lab/loompy/issues/1#issuecomment-346485487) all that we need for backing AnnData objects, we currently base this on a very similar hdf5-format (categorical data types, arbitrary unstructured annotations, hdf5 sparse datasets), but will be happy to move away from this, once loom can fully back AnnData objects.

PS: A revised version of the Scanpy draft will appear in Genome Biology very soon and we reference and acknowledge the  discussion here.
PPS: I haven't been able to post this here or at another place earlier because my wife and I just got twins... 😄  So, I will be slow in responding. Maybe @flying-sheep can help out here and there, regarding anndata and Scanpy...

Overwriting the `info` dict is intended behavior, but it is obviously really bad for your usecase.
Resuming experiments seems like a common usecase, and I agree that we need a better way of doing that, so I'll mark this as a feature request.

We could provide an option to resume an experiment by id, such as `--resume 47`. Observers could then use that to append to the respective `info` and metrics. 
I can see a few problems with that:
1) how to handle differences in the rest of the entry? Maybe one of the dependencies was upgraded, or the code changed. Or even the config. Should Sacred refuse to continue in this case? That seems to be a good safeguard against mistakenly continuing the wrong experiment. But what if you had to fix a minor problem in the code, or update some library because of a bug? Maybe we would also need a `--force-resume` option?
2) Most code cannot be interrupted and continued at any point, but instead relies on checkpoints. What to do with `info` entries and metrics between the checkpoint and the interruption? They would be duplicated in an naive implementation. Maybe sacred should have a way of storing the times of the last checkpoint, so that `--resume` would overwrite all entries after that checkpoint time? 

I'd love to hear your thoughts on this.

I think what you want to do is very similar to a forward data-flow analysis: for each basic block in the function, you take an input state, feed it to angr's execution engine (SimuVEX), and take the output state. The output state will become an input state of the corresponding successor of the last block. Theory-wise, you can refer to [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Data-flow_analysis).

[This analysis](https://github.com/angr/angr/blob/feat/variable_recovery/angr/analyses/variable_recovery/variable_recovery.py) as well as [its base analysis](https://github.com/angr/angr/blob/feat/variable_recovery/angr/analyses/forward_analysis.py) demonstrates a basic skeleton of a simple forward data-flow analysis. You can use that as a reference. Note that they are not in master branch (yet), but the basic ideas are the same.

Hey Samacumen, your setup looks good and my guess is that this issue is larger than your use of DroneKit.  Can you confirm whether you still get the serial port error when just using mavutil vice DroneKit to open and read data from the pixhawk you've got attached to the pi?  Try reading/writing to the device returned from mavutil.mavlink_connection(device="/dev/ttyAMA0", baud=115200) and see if you get the same serial port issue. 

One problem I found when using the Pi as a companion computer was the Pi's ability to supply enough current to USB devices.  For instance, when I was using both a Pixhawk and a GSM modem on the Pi's USB ports, it would overwhelm the Pi's ability to supply enough current and cause serial port issues.  Just be aware that the Pi (by default) only supplies 600 milliamps to the USB ports.  You can "amp up" the current supplied to the Pi's USB ports to 1.2 amps by editing the file /boot/config.txt and adding the line max_usb_current=1 to the end of the file.  Of course, make sure that the AC adapter that is powering your Pi (and by extension also powering everything on your Pi's USB ports) supplies enough current in the first place.  I want to say my wall plug supplies like 2 amps to my Pi, but I'm not at my bench to check.

For the new functionality of the auto flusher that @milliams is going to add, a request to flush will be picked up and carried out (locks permitting) within a second so you won't have to wait 30s for it. The main reason I would very much prefer the queue idea is that we restrict flushes to just one place in the code and as the flush was the main source of deadlocks, this makes everything a lot cleaner. This also has the added benefit of pushing the (possibly very expensive) flush to the background and not holding up the user, i.e. j.submit goes even quicker :smile:

There is an additional question of do we actually want to flush half way through submission though. I would argue this wouldn't help and could do harm because you would saving the job in an inconsistent state that you couldn't do much with afterwards. Requesting a flush at the start and at the end should really be enough I feel.

Having said all that, with the code as we have it, direct flushing in submit is definitely a good idea and I withdraw my previous comment as the PR passes the tests and thinking about it I don't **think** it could cause deadlocks as it stands :smile:

ofxRSSDK is an openFrameworks wrapper for the RealSense SDK on Windows (the production software stack for app developers). It will not compile or work on OSX as the official Intel SDK _only_ works on Windows. librealsense is a separate camera capture driver (designed specifically for OSX and Linux users). The example code you need exists as one of the projects in the samples/ directory. 

Probably what you want to do is create an openFrameworks app like normal, and then add the librealsense XCode project to your workspace. XCode will automatically figure out that the librealsense project is a dependency and link it properly. Then, you can rip out some of the librealsense sample code and insert that into your OFX app, modulo anything that has to do with GLFW, because ofx makes & handles your window and GLFW is not needed.

My thoughts around context in Mycroft are jelling as I get to know this codebase better and think about what I had implemented in Christopher (the technology I built before Mycroft).  In Christopher, there were several types of context that I will be integrating:

* Recent history context
Just knowing what Skills have been invoked or interacted with recently is a powerful thing.  For example, the phrase "stop it" can easily be interpreted if you know that an Alarm skill triggered a buzzer a few seconds ago.  My implementation was to track not just the immediately Skill history, but everything invoked in the previous 5 minutes.  The Skills in that recent list got a preview of all the utterances from the speaker, allowing them to handle phrases that wouldn't make any sense without context.  That allows sensible handling of overlapping vocabulary, also.  For example, "how about Thursday" could be handled by either a Weather Forecast or a Calendar skill.  The recent history context allows the correct Skill to be selected.

* Conversation context
The concept of a "conversation" implies some memory of what was said or done before.  In Christopher I allow system responses to also include a packet of "context" info that will be delivered to the Skill the next time it is invoked.  The Skill writer can use that context to better understand the utterance.  I used this technique to build a sort of enhanced phone-tree system, where you can define structures of interactions and branches based on the response.  It is handy when working through a multi-step procedure.  But you can also use the context a little more free-form, perhaps just recalling who you sent a texts to recently so that the phrase "Tell Joe I'm going to be late" could figure out on its own that "Joe" is "Joe Smith" who you texted a few minutes ago, not "Joe Jones" or "Joe Johnson" who you haven't interacted with in months.  And that you were texting his work number, not his personal phone.

* Audible/Visual context
Christopher allows control of an associated display (e.g. a nearby TV).  What is showing on that TV is part of the context the person is experiencing in that room.  So if I pull up a recipe on that TV and have it show the preparation steps, the simple phrase "next step" can be understood.  I implemented this by giving the Skill that created the current onscreen display the same preview of utterances.

* Location context
Along with the words spoken, we know about the context of the location where they were said.  So, for example, saying "turn on the lights" would have two different results if said to the Mycroft unit with the name "Bedroom" than they would to the unit named "Kitchen".  All Skill should have access to location info when handling utterances.

* User context
There are also several techniques to distinguish registered speakers, so "what is on my agenda for today" would react differently for me than for my daughter.  

* Changes in presence
I have a few things in Christopher that I'll be pulling into Mycroft before long.  One involves using personal Bluetooth devices (Fitbit or phone) to identify who is in the room without them even saying anything.  Skills will be able to register to be notified of user arrival/departures.  Christopher uses this to remind me of upcoming calendar events when I walk in the house, to turn room lights on when I walk in the room, and to turn lights off when I leave the house.


One tricky part in migrating this into Mycroft is how Adapt maps utterances to intent.  I don't believe analyzing the words of the utterance alone can determine the intent.  Instead I think it will be necessary to match likely intent Skill targets and give them all a chance to determine how confident they are that the utterance was directed at them.  Then the system can either take the highest confidence Skill response or perhaps ask the user for clarification if there are two near confidence matches resulting in ambiguity.  (I hadn't resolved the ambiguity issue in Christopher yet, I just took the highest confidence which worked really well on its own.)

This is completely doable using Adapt, but requires breaking some of the expectations of Skill writers and/or adding new capabilities.  For example, Skills will need something like a handle_conversation(self, utterance) function that might be invoked when they are not the target of the conversation.   E.g. the LightsSkill might receive "how about tomorrow" because the user turned on the light recently and got it in the Recent list.  But the phrase was really directed at the WeatherSkill which the user had invoked right after turning on the lights.

I hope that helps, feel free to discuss and give feedback!

   - Steve

The TensorFlow `FileWriter` will always include the actual timestamp as part of the file name. We don't plan to change that.

Why do you want all the events to be stored in one event file?

Regardless of whether your events are all in one file, TensorBoard will not be able to visualize your data properly, because for each step you have multiple conflicting events. TensorBoard expects that there is at most one event for each tag at each step.

You have two options, depending on what you're trying to do and what your samples mean. Suppose that at Time A you serialize ten steps (`len(rwd_avg_train) == 10`) and then you do the same at Time B. Does the first sample at Time B come _after_ the first sample at Time A (are your samples sequential?), or do they represent _independent_ runs (perhaps each invocation of the code that you posted corresponds to a set of hyperparameter values?)?

If the former, then you simply need to set your `step` correctly: instead of `for step in range(len(rwd_avg_train))`, something like

```py
next_step = 0
for i in range(len(rwd_avg_train)):
  step = step_base + i 
  summary = tf.Summary(value=[
    tf.Summary.Value(tag="rwd_avg_train", simple_value=rwd_avg_train[i]),
    …,
  ])
  summary_writer.add_summary(summary, step)
step_base += len(rwd_avg_train)
```
Note that in this case there is no reason to create a new summary writer at each invocation.

If the latter, then you should create a separate summary writer for each run:
```py
run = 0  # initialized somewhere...

run += 1
path = os.path.join('/home/admin/event', 'run_%04d' % run)
summary_writer = tf.summary.FileWriter(path)

for step in range(len(rwd_avg_train)):
  # as in your code
```

I agree the security checks are lacking.  I plan to put a whitelist check in place where unsecure frames MUST:

1. Must be a 6LoWPAN frame that matches either 6LoWPAN IPHC or Fragment Header types.
2. IPv6 message length is limited to maximum required for MLE and Thread Commissioning.
3. IPv6 source and destination addresses MUST have link-local scope.
4. IPv6 Next Header MUST be UDP.
5. UDP Destination Port MUST match MLE UDP port and Joiner Port for Thread Commissioning.

It would be nice to also limit the total allocation of receive message buffers for unsecure frames, but it is difficult to know what a good limit is without some empirical data.

Thoughts?

Yikes, looks like I missed a lot of this conversation. There is a lot going on in this thread, so take this as my response to the first pass:

I think the goal of having all OpenThread NCP devices be able to also support running the stack in the host is a reasonable and achievable goal without adding much additional stuff to the NCP. Honestly, there only seems to be one thing missing to enable a host-side stack: the ability to send raw frames. Everything else should "just work".

Regarding @pvanhorn's recent argument:

> Depending on how tight resources are you could define a function that the 'other' module must implement. This eliminates the registration logic and allows for a direct function call without pointers. Then if you have multiple modules which act as customers you would have that one function mux to the others. That solution would offer the most efficient mechanism for the single mode systems and only require extra code for the multi-mode solution.

To the extent that OpenThread is intended to minimally run on 32-bit ARM-based microcontrollers, the additional overhead of callback registration and function pointer indirection is minimal. If the additional overhead were actually a consideration, we wouldn't be using `virtual` all over the place. So let's ignore that argument: It's a red herring.

The real reason I think so many people are concerned is that it will require changes on the Nest side, which is annoying—especially when there is no obvious benefit to Nest. But really, it isn't that much work to update our internal platforms to use a dynamic callback instead of a static callback. It is a better API pattern in general, so we should have a good reason if we are doing otherwise for new APIs (rather than the other way around).

All that being said, I think we can achieve the above mentioned immediate goal of enabling a host-side stack *without changing existing APIs* by *adding* an OpenThread API for sending raw 802.15.4 frames. This sidesteps the whole changing-the-PHY-API argument, while also enabling the ability to send raw frames while OpenThread is running: which is useful.

The only really tricky part is serving sleepy-end-devices (due to timing requirements), but we can cross that bridge when we get to it.

The 'direct to radio' interface would be independent of the otPlatRadio interface.  The two interfaces should have no inter-dependencies as one should be able to exist without the other.  First and foremost it provides an interface that Spinel can use to facilitate its radio needs in lieu of OT resident on device.  Secondly, it provides this interface inspite of OT also being present (to satisfy Microsoft's needs).

There might need to be some checks in Spinel to ensure that this interface is not used while OT is active in order to avoid contention for the same underlying resource. I would imagine that if otEnable were called through Spinel that this 'direct to radio' interface, if present, would need to be disabled.

The feature set would need to be conditionally compiled in/out to avoid unnecessary code bloat on systems that don't require this feature.

I finally have found a solution to this problem. There is a library made by Google which has everything we need to consume Firestore's RPC API using .NET. You don't need to build a gRPC client from scratch. The library Google.Cloud.Firestore.Data is the way to go. It is built on top of .NET Standard (currently netstandard 1.5), and it is currently in pre-alpha stage.

Google has not published this package to Nuget yet. If you want to consume the library, you can download the entire Visual Studio solution from [Google Cloud Libraries for .NET repository](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-dotnet/tree/master/apis/Google.Cloud.Firestore.Data). Note that currently Google.Cloud.Firestore.Data is not listed in the README, but you can find the source code under apis/Google.Cloud.Firestore.Data.

There is also a documentation being developed by Google which can be found on [this link](https://jskeet.github.io/google-cloud-dotnet/docs/Google.Cloud.Firestore.Data/index.html). The documentation provides all information you need to consume the library.

Basic ideas/assumptions of this implementation:
- re-use SMOTE implementation for continuous features
- assume nominal features are one-hot encoded
- compute median std of continuous features as proposed in paper
- plug it in where nominal one-hot encoded features are equal to 1, thus making sure distance is calculated as expected whenever k-neighbors is invoked -> original X is replaced with a modified copy
- generate new sample using normal SMOTE and then replace nominal features as suggested in paper

@BoPeng perfect summary! I would say that there is a place for both approaches. I see the benefits of SoS tasks in many situations and we will definitely use them in parallel to remote_ikernel solution.

We have visited this topic a few times and the center problem is how to maintain the server (redis or not). I would rule out the possibility for requiring users to start a server manually, so we have either 

1. A single server solution: sos query a server, connect to an existing one or starts one if no server is available. The server is shared by multiple sos instances so it should not be killed with the death of an sos instance, but it should kill itself if no one talks to it for a while.

2. A multi-server solution: each sos instance starts a server with a different port. The problem with this approach is that multiple-servers need to talk if the server is supposed to keep track of signatures, task status etc. This can be difficult to do as each server could cache information to itself, otherwise sharing a database has its own problems (servers lock database to write etc).

The server does not have to be a redis server, because we do not have to use redis database to store the information. For example, we can use a simple dictionary to keep track of worker status and use the server to coordinate the execution of workers. We could migrate things like file signature to the server but there are practical problems. For example,

1. We might not want to start a server for task execution because tasks could be sent to different computing nodes so file system is the only way to read/write signatures. 
2. We might not be able to use the server for file locking as well because file lock is released when the process is killed but this is difficult to mimic in a server implementation.

I think what I will do is start a zeroMQ branch and implement a on-demand server process. We can then use the server to do more and more things.

`step_output` is by design the output of the entire step. I would even say it was incorrect to make it available to the step itself because an input group is not supposed to know the output of other groups. We ended up doing something very complicated with this variable (accumulative of `_output` and adjust `step_output` if an input group fails), and it would have been easier if we simply provide `step_output` only to other steps.
This is not possible given our existing way of implementation... `sos_run` is even disabled for steps with `concurrent=True` (#901).
An alternative is to by default add `name` to `tags` so that we can tell from job id from the task id. In the current implementation, it is difficult to link pbs jobs and sos tasks.

This PR is intended to make the dataset tests pass on windows. Specifically, an issue on windows is that closing the experiment connection to the database will not close all connections to the database, as the dataset instance has an independent connection. Since on Windows we cannot remove a file which is still accessed by a process, this connection will cause tests which use the "experiment" fixture to potentially fail. This is because calling "e.conn.close()" will not close *all* connections. 

A solution is to make the dataset and the experiment use the same connection.

That's a good point.
I checked in some major image libraries in python, and they don't have a consensus either. Here is what they do for image resize for example:

| Lib          | Format |
|--------------|--------|
| PIL          | (W, H) |
| OpenCV       | (W, H) |
| scipy.misc   | (H, W) |
| scikit-image | (H, W) |

Let's go for the (H, W), to make it more consistent with the image ordering (and also it makes it easier to get the dimensions right, e.g., `tensor.size()[2:]`). As a little plus, TF also uses (H, W) for it's image operations.
